coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent sensor array constructed by functionalized carbon nanodots for qualitative and quantitative analysis of urinary organic acids biomarkers
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Na
Chen, Manling
Cui, Yunmiao
Liu, Xue
Li, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Adequate assessment of the level of organic acids (OA) in human urine is significant for non-invasive diagnosis and monitoring of OA-related diseases. However, the common lock-and-key sampling method for specific OA analysis is dramatically perturbed by the complicated chemical components in urine. A comprehensive qualitative and quantitative analysis of OAs in urine samples remains a significant challenge. Herein, a cross-reactive fluorescent sensor array based on amino acid-functionalized carbon nanodots (CDs) was designed for simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of seven kinds of OAs, including homovanillic acid (HVA), vanillylmandelic acid (VMA), 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid (3-HA), lactic acid (LA), pyruvic acid (PA), glutaric acid (GA), and methylmalonic acid (MMA). The array system can specifically recognize multiple OAs in urine samples over a wide concentrations range (0.1–1000 μM) with 100% accuracy. The wide concentration range and high accuracy of the CD array system for OA analysis depend on a distinct fluorescence response from the differential binding affinity of various OAs to the CDs. The excellent recognition ability and selectivity of the sensing system were further confirmed by the double-blind and interference experiments. The sensor array achieved satisfactory distinguishing effects of separate OAs and binary OA samples (HVA/VMA) in urine samples. In particular, the quantitative analysis of the one-OA system or two-OA system (HVA/VMA) can be realized by the array system. These results confirm that the sensor array based on the CDs is an efficient platform for OA analysis and a convenient tool for the non-invasive diagnosis of OA-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor array
Organic acids
Carbon nanodots
Urine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Identification of OAs in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51873085 ) and LiaoNing Revitalization Talents Program ( XLYC2007056 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B, C) TEM images of S1–S3. (D, E, F) PL spectra of S1–S3 under different excitation wavelengths.
Deconvoluted high-resolution XPS of S1–S3 for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and B 1s spectra. (A) S1; (B) S2; (C) S3.

(A) Canonical score plot for response patterns of all analytes at different concentrations. Seven data points with the same color represent all analytes at the same concentration. (B) Mean Euclidean distance (ED) values of all analytes at different concentrations. (C) Canonical score plot for response patterns obtained against 500 nM of OAs. (D) Identification of unknown OAs using an as-prepared sensor array. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fingerprint of OAs (500 nM) and other interfering substances (50 μM). Each value is the average of five independent measurements; each error bar shows the standard deviation of these measurements. (B) Canonical score plot for response patterns obtained against OAs (500 nM) and other interfering substances (50 μM).

(A) Canonical score plot for response patterns obtained against OAs (500 nM) in urine. (B) Canonical score plot for response patterns obtained against the mixtures of HVA and VMA with different molar ratios (total OA concentration was 5 μM) in urine.

(A, B) Canonical score plot for analysis of urinary VMA (A) and HVA (B) at different concentrations. (C, D) Linear calibration model for quantitative analysis of VMA (C) and HVA (D) (PC: prediction concentration; PER: prediction error rate).
Simultaneous quantitative analysis of HVA and VMA in the two-component VMA-HVA samples in urine using the SVM algorithm.
The working mechanism of the fluorescent sensor array is based on the CDs for efficient analysis of organic acids.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent sensor array constructed by functionalized carbon nanodots for qualitative and quantitative analysis of urinary organic acids biomarkers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, Liaoning University, Shenyang, Liaoning 110036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, Liaoning University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, Liaoning University, Shenyang, Liaoning 110036, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Cell Biology, Key Laboratory of Cell Biology of Ministry of Public Health, Key Laboratory of Medical Cell Biology of Ministry of Education, China Medical University, Shenyang, Liaoning 110122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Cell Biology, Key Laboratory of Cell Biology of Ministry of Public Health, Key Laboratory of Medical Cell Biology of Ministry of Education, China Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Cell Biology, Key Laboratory of Cell Biology of Ministry of Public Health, Key Laboratory of Medical Cell Biology of Ministry of Education, China Medical University, 110122, Shenyang, Liaoning, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Na Du and Manling Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adequate assessment of the level of organic acids (OA) in human urine is significant for non-invasive diagnosis and monitoring of OA-related diseases. However, the common lock-and-key sampling method for specific OA analysis is dramatically perturbed by the complicated chemical components in urine. A comprehensive qualitative and quantitative analysis of OAs in urine samples remains a significant challenge. Herein, a cross-reactive fluorescent sensor array based on amino acid-functionalized carbon nanodots (CDs) was designed for simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of seven kinds of OAs, including homovanillic acid (HVA), vanillylmandelic acid (VMA), 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid (3-HA), lactic acid (LA), pyruvic acid (PA), glutaric acid (GA), and methylmalonic acid (MMA). The array system can specifically recognize multiple OAs in urine samples over a wide concentrations range (0.1–1000 μM) with 100% accuracy. The wide concentration range and high accuracy of the CD array system for OA analysis depend on a distinct fluorescence response from the differential binding affinity of various OAs to the CDs. The excellent recognition ability and selectivity of the sensing system were further confirmed by the double-blind and interference experiments. The sensor array achieved satisfactory distinguishing effects of separate OAs and binary OA samples (HVA/VMA) in urine samples. In particular, the quantitative analysis of the one-OA system or two-OA system (HVA/VMA) can be realized by the array system. These results confirm that the sensor array based on the CDs is an efficient platform for OA analysis and a convenient tool for the non-invasive diagnosis of OA-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic acids (OAs) are primary, intermediate metabolites of cellular macromolecules and are enriched in the urine [1]. Abnormal OAs in urine are often related to the inborn errors of metabolism or the onset of certain specific diseases, such as pheochromocytoma and neuroblastoma [2,3]. Urinary OAs can be employed as biomarkers to monitor the pathological status of multiple metabolic pathways and non-invasively diagnose OA-related diseases. Developing comprehensive and practical analysis methods of urinary OAs is critical for clinical research and public health. Common OA analysis methods are chromatographic, electrophoretic, and extractive analysis [4–6]. These methods depend on expensive pieces of equipment and professional operators. A complicated derivatization process is necessary for OA analysis, owing to their high polarity, low volatility, and high tendency towards dehydration. In order to conquer the above drawbacks, many efforts have been made to develop novel sensing technologies based on sensors with various sensing signals for practical OA analysis. These sensing signals can come from optical spectroscopy, surface plasmon resonance, or electrochemistry [7–14]. Most of these sensors are designed based on a lock-and-key principle for a specific OA target. The lock-and-key sensors have the advantages of specificity and sensitivity.

However, in the clinical diagnosis of OA-related diseases, multiple OA biomarkers should be combined for an accurate diagnosis, and there is a relationship between their concentration and related disorders [15–19]. For instance, the high content of urinary vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) is a significant feature of pheochromocytoma, but a simultaneous increase in urinary VMA and homovanillic acid (HVA) content is an essential indicator for clinical diagnosis of neuroblastoma [20,21]. For neuroblastoma, the diagnostic cutoff values of urinary HVA and VMA were 34.45 μM and 11.2 μM, respectively [15]. Hence, an accurate diagnosis for these two diseases requires a conjoint analysis of VMA and HVA. There is an urgent need to develop effective methodologies for portable, real-time, and sensitive identification of multiple OAs in the urine.

Array-based sensing system assembled from various cross-reactive sensors has emerged as a powerful tool for simultaneously identifying multiple analytes in the complex environment [22,23]. A growing number of multichannel array-based sensing systems have been designed and applied to recognize a list of given analytes, such as organic or inorganic anions, biomolecules, pathogenic bacteria, cells [23–28]. Some studies have been about applying array-based sensing systems for OA detection [4,29–38]. However, most of them can only qualitatively identify and discriminate OAs, and few studies can achieve a simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of multiple urinary OAs. Quantitative analysis can help determine urinary organic acids' content and support the early and precise diagnosis of OA-related diseases. Besides, many chemical ingredients exist in the urine samples, such as amino acids, urea, and inorganic salts [39,40]. These interferents also increase the difficulty in precise recognition of OA targets and restriction the sensitivity and accuracy of OA analysis in practical applications. Hence, there is a pressing demand to exploit a rational multichannel sensing platform for the simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of OA biomarkers in a complicated urine environment.

In this work, we constructed a multichannel fluorescent sensing platform using three kinds of functionalized carbon nanodots (CDs) for simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of urinary OA biomarkers. HVA, VMA, and a set of typical OA biomarkers of organic aciduria were detected to estimate the performance of the sensor array. These CD sensors can interact directly with OA biomarkers without the need for intermedia through electrostatic and covalent bonding. The high-throughput output signal can be fed back to form differential signal data for further array analysis of various OA targets ( Scheme 1). This direct-response sensing system can effectively avoid the signal interference caused by the impurities in the urine sample and improve the stability and maximize the precision in OA analysis. The sensing system can differentiate seven targets in the concentration range from 0.1 to 1000 μM with 100% accuracy. The excellent performance of the sensing system was further confirmed by the double-blind and interference experiments. Besides, the facile multi-dimensional sensing platform realized satisfactory discrimination of OAs and VMA-HVA samples in urine samples. Of note, the sensing platform can quantitatively detect VMA or HVA within one-component or two-component OA systems. These results illustrated that the sensor array is promising for the simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of multiple urinary OA biomarkers in practical applications.
The reagents, details of CDs characterization, and calculation of fluorescence quantum yields (QY) are provided in the Supplementary Material.

All of the CDs were synthesized by using 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid (3-APBA) as the carbon precursor through a one-step hydrothermal process with L-glycine (Gly), L-alanine (Ala), and L-valine (Val) as the co-reactant, respectively. Briefly, 10 mmol of 3-APBA was separately mixed with 20 mmol of Gly, Ala, Val, and mixtures dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. The solution was then transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 8 h. After that, the product naturally cooled to room temperature was filtered by filtration (PES filter membrane with 0.22 µm pore size) to remove insoluble impurities. The unreacted reactants and precursors were removed for 2days through dialyzing with a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000) in ultrapure water. The three purified were collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The absorbance (A) of all CDs solution was regulated to 0.1 at their optimum excitation wavelength for minimizing the concentration effect of CDs. Then, the equal volume of OA solutions with different concentrations was added respectively into 5 mL CD solutions to give final OAs concentrations in the range of 0.1–1000 μM, and the fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded. The 5 repeated trials of each analyte interacting with different sensor units were actualized under the same condition. The FL response patterns ((I0-I)/I0) were obtained by normalizing the collected data, where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of CDs at their optimum emission wavelength in the absence and presence of OAs. The raw data matrix (3 sensors×7 OAs×5 replicates) was obtained and further estimated by linear discriminant analysis (LDA) in SPSS v22.0. Unless otherwise specified, all solutions were prepared with ultrapure water, as well in the following experiments.

Three kinds of OAs selected from seven targets randomly as unknown samples were treated by the sensor array. 5 μL unknown sample solutions (500 μM) were measured and added to 5 mL CD solutions, respectively. The final concentrations of three kinds of unknown samples in the test solutions were 500 nM. Five repeated experiments were performed for each test sample, and the recorded FL response signals were further normalized ((I0-I)/I0). The data point coordinates of unknown samples were obtained by substituting the normalized data into the formula derived from the initial training matrix (qualitative differentiation of all OAs at 500 nM). The unknown samples were finally determined by calculating and comparing the Mahalanobis distance between the unknown and the initial training samples.

Some main chemical components of urine (Gly, L-Histidine (His), L-Serine (Ser), Urea, NH4Cl, NaCl, Na2HCO3, Na2SO4, and KH2PO4) were chosen as interfering substances. 5 μL OA and interferents solutions with specific concentrations (OA solutions=500 μM and interferents solutions=50 mM) were added separately into 5 mL CD solutions, and the final concentrations of analytes and interferents in test samples were 500 nM and 50 μM, respectively. The FL response data were normalized ((I0-I)/I0) and further estimated by LDA in SPSS v22.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of OAs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: All OA solutions were prepared by dissolving various OAs in morning urine diluted with ultrapure water 5 times. A series of urinary OA test samples were prepared by adding 5 μL OA solutions (500 μM) into 5 mL CD solutions, respectively. The FL spectra of CD solutions and urinary OA test samples were recorded and normalized. The normalized FL response data ((I0-I)/I0) were evaluated by LDA in SPSS v22.0. When discriminating two-component samples of urinary OAs, the VMA-HVA solutions with different molar ratios were added respectively into 5 mL CD solution, and the total OA concentration in test samples was 5 μM. The FL response data were collected and further analyzed by LDA in SPSS 22.0 after normalization.

VMA/HVA solutions were prepared by dissolving OAs in morning urine diluted with ultrapure water 5 times. For quantitative analysis of unary OA, six concentrations (1 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM, 80 μM, 150 μM and 200 μM) selected as standard samples were detected for establishing the standard curve of OA analysis, and two concentrations (60 μM and 100 μM) chosen as test samples were utilized to evaluate the standard curve. 5 repeated trials under each concentration were actualized, and LDA processed the FL response data after normalization. Subsequently, seven sets of VMA-HVA two-component samples with various concentrations were prepared for quantitative VMA/HVA in two-component samples. Five replicates and data normalization were actualized, and these results were further processed by LDA in SPSS v22.0 and support vector machine (SVM) in MATLAB v9.3.

Three CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal methods. The CDs prepared from Gly, Ala, and Val were labeled as Gly-CDs (S1), Ala-CDs (S2), and Val-CDs (S3). Fig. 1A–C show that all the CDs are spherical and well dispersed from each other. The average size of S1 is about 5 nm, and that of both S2 and S3 is about 4 nm, respectively. There are two absorption maxima at 235 nm and 290 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs (Fig. S1), which are attributed to the π–π* transition of the conjugate CC/CN band and the n–π* transition of the N- and O-containing functional groups [41,42]. The PL spectra of the CDs under various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1D−F) show that three CDs have strong PL emission at 375 nm with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. The fluorescence QY of these three CDs was calculated to be 36.69% (S1), 36.09% (S2), and 36.87% (S3) using quinine sulfate as the references, respectively. These results confirmed that the successful synthesis of the CDs with efficient fluorescence emission.

The chemical structure and surface functional groups of the CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. S2) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S3 and Fig. 2). These two characterization methods confirmed the existence of rich hydrophilic functional groups and boronic-acid groups on the surface of the CDs. FT-IR spectra show that the characteristic absorption peaks of stretching vibration for O−H/N−H and CO are at about 3450 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1, respectively. The distinct absorption bands at 1360, 1190, and 1090 cm−1 are attributed to B−O stretching vibration, B−OH bending vibration, C−B stretching vibration [43,44]. The C 1s band is deconvoluted into the C−B (283.6 eV), C−C/CC (284.3 eV), C−N (285.0 eV), C−O (286.0 eV), and CO/CN (288.0 eV); the deconvolution of N 1s spectra illustrate four relative nitrogen species of pyridinic N (398.5 eV), amino N (400.0 eV), pyrrolic N (400.9 eV), and graphitic N (401.6 eV); the deconvolution of O 1s spectra reveal that oxygen atoms in the form of CO (531.5 eV), C-O (532.3 eV), and B-O (533.4 eV). Besides, B 1s analysis revealed that the boron atoms are connected in C−B (191.8 eV) and B−O (192.7 eV).

The multichannel fluorescent sensor array was constructed by three CDs synthesized from 3-APBA and three different amino acids. The excellent identification ability of the sensing system to OAs is mainly attributed to the differential interaction between the sensing units and OAs (Scheme 1). The CDs can combine with different OAs by electrostatic interaction, and the binding OAs will affect the protonation degree of the CDs and their PL behavior [45,46]. The protonation degree of the CDs depends on the type and content of their surficial functional groups and OAs. Doping of different amino acids varies the type and the content of hydrophilic functional groups on the CDs (e.g., carboxyl and amine groups), as revealed by the XPS results. Different OAs present various solution pKa and result in a differential protonation ability for the CDs. For instance, OAs with low pKa (high acidity conditions) tend to protonate the amino-functional groups and deprotonate carboxyl-functional groups on the CDs. Besides, the boric acid functional groups on the CDs can covalently bind to the α-hydroxyl carboxylic acids (e.g., VMA and LA) [47,48]. The electrostatic and covalent binding will bury the FL emission centers of the CDs originated from the edge states and distinct functional groups and weaken the FL emission of the CDs [49]. To further demonstrate the differential binding between the CDs and OAs, we titrated different CDs with different OAs in ultrapure water and obtained the binding constants (log K
SV ) using a modified Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)). The log K
SV values for these complexes are diverse in the range of 1.8–3.4, revealing the different recognition and combination ability of the CDs for different OAs. The larger the log K
SV value is, the stronger the binding ability of the CDs to OAs is. Table 1 shows the relationship between the binding constant and the pKa value of OAs. Almost all the log K
SV values increase with the decrease of the pKa values of OAs but VMA and LA. Both VMA and LA are α-hydroxyl carboxylic acids, and they can covalently bind with the boric acid functional groups of the CDs. The electrostatic and covalent bonding synergistically make the binding between the CDs and VMA or LA stronger than the other ones. These characteristic structure factors of the CDs and OAs contribute to differentiate data results and favor array analysis. Different OAs can combine with different CDs and quench their fluorescence to varying degrees, outputting differential fluorescence signal patterns for array analysis. Accordingly, the discrimination or pattern recognition of multiple OAs can be achieved through statistical analysis of these differential fluorescence responses by LDA.

S1, S2, and S3 were combined to construct the sensor array. Seven kinds of OAs, including HVA, VMA, pyruvic acid (PA), lactic acid (LA), glutaric acid (GA), methylmalonic acid (MMA), and 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid (3-HA), were chosen as analytes to test the analysis ability of the sensor array. Firstly, we differentiated all OAs at eight typical concentrations in the range of 0–1000 μM to evaluate the concentration effect of OAs on the sensor array. The different binding affinities between OAs and CDs caused a unique fluorescent response and generated a three-signal pattern. The fluorescent response patterns of the training matrix (3 sensors×7 OAs×5 replicates) were further analyzed and visualized by the LDA. The analysis results ( Fig. 3A) showed that the distribution of data points became increasingly diverse with the increase of OA concentration. This demonstrated that the discriminating ability of the sensing system for OAs would be enhanced in a high concentration range.

Furthermore, the discriminating ability of the sensing system for OAs was evaluated by the mean Euclidean distance (ED) of data points at respective concentrations. The ED between various data points at the same concentration was calculated, and the mean of them was plotted in Fig. 3B. The larger the mean ED value is, the greater the dispersion of these data is, and the better the discriminating ability of the sensor array is. With the increase of OA concentration, the ED values tend to be larger, indicating a more efficient discriminative effect of the sensor array.

The discriminative effect of the sensor array for OAs was evaluated at two minimum concentrations (100 nM and 500 nM) after multiple trials. From Fig. 3A, the data points from the same concentrations (100 nM and 500 nM) look aggregate compared with those from high concentrations of OAs. However, the data clusters of each OA can be separated from each other at both two concentrations (Fig. 3C and Fig. S4). This demonstrates that the detecting and discriminating ability of the sensing system for OAs can reach 100 nM, which is far lower than that of the other sensor arrays for OAs (Table S1). The data points of five independent measurements form closer clusters at 500 nM compared to those at 100 nM. This suggests that 100 nM is very close to the detection limit of the sensing system.

Furthermore, double-blind experiments were performed to evaluate the accuracy and practicability of the multichannel sensing platform. Three unknown samples (UN-1, UN-2, and UN-3) were tested by the sensing platform, and the corresponding normalized FL responses were further analyzed by LDA based on the initial training matrix. As shown in Fig. 3D, all blind samples were correctly defined as their respective corresponding species (UN-1 =HVA, UN-2 =VMA, and UN-3 =LA) according to the shortest Mahalanobis distance principle. These results demonstrate a satisfying recognition and discriminative performance of the developed sensor array for OAs.

The selectivity and specificity of the sensor array are essential for their practical application. An interference experiment was designed by introducing some urine-based interferents into the detection solution, such as Gly, His, Ser, Urea, NH4Cl, NaCl, Na2HCO3, Na2SO4, and KH2PO4 (all at 50 μM). The fingerprint ( Fig. 4A) shows no obvious fluorescence response with the addition of interfering substances, even if the concentration of the interfering substance is 100 times higher than that of OAs. Fig. 4B intuitively shows the distinctive effects of the sensor array on analytes and interferents. High concentrations of interferents hardly affect the identification of OAs in analysis. The results confirm the specificity and anti-interference capability of the sensing platform for OAs. This makes the sensing platform appropriate for OA analysis in real samples.

In order to evaluate the feasibility and applicability in real samples, the functionalized CD-based fluorescent sensing platform was employed for OA analysis in human urine. As shown in Fig. 5A, the sensing system also performed well in discriminating seven kinds of OAs in urine samples. The specific interaction of these sensor elements with analytes contributed to the realization of satisfactory OA discrimination and confirmed the utility and stability of the sensing system.

The discrimination of coexisting OAs is a challenge since different concentrations and molar ratios of analytes may alter the response patterns. Urinary HVA/VMA ratio as a screening tool can contribute to the early diagnosis of Menkes syndrome (MNK) [50]. MNK can affect the normal copper levels in the body and lead to growth and psychomotor disorders. Hence, the VMA-HVA two-component samples in urine were chosen as the model to study further the excellent determination ability towards OA mixture analysis based on the sensor array. A series of two-component samples with different molar ratios (total OA concentration was 5 μM) were prepared and treated with the sensing system. As shown in Fig. 5B, all two-component samples, as well as pure HVA and VMA, were clearly distinguished from each other without any overlap. All 50 cases (10 samples×5 replicates) were correctly assigned to their respective clusters with satisfied dispersion of 100% total variance.

Urinary VMA and HVA levels cannot only support early diagnosis of MNK, but they are also correlated with the stage of the disease, management, and prognosis from patients with pheochromocytoma and neuroblastoma [51]. Quantitative analysis of VMA/HVA levels in urine is of great significance for the accurate diagnosis of these diseases. The standard and test samples were prepared and analyzed by the sensor array to evaluate the quantitative analysis of urinary VMA/HVA. Canonical score plot of urinary VMA/HVA analysis shows that the FL response pattern has an association with VMA/HVA concentrations. Take VMA as an example ( Fig. 6A); the VMA standard samples (solid data points) from different concentrations present a series of distinctly separate clusters. They are distributed from left to right with the increase of VMA concentration. This concentration-dependent distribution can be attributed to the concentration-dependent FL quenching of the CDs. The standard samples of HVA exhibit a similar distribution pattern to that of VMA (Fig. 6B). The samples to be tested were treated with the sensing array, and the FL response patterns were estimated by the initial algorithm derived from the LDA analysis of the standard samples. As shown in Fig. 6A,B, all of the data points from the test samples (hollow data points) fall in the correct concentration range, both for VMA and HVA. For instance, the data points with the concentration of 60 μM are located between the data points of the standard samples with the concentrations of 40 μM and 80 μM. In order to quantify VMA and HVA levels in the urine samples, the FL response patterns from the standard and the selected samples under different concentrations were further converted to the EDs. Fig. 6C,D shows that the ED-dose curve from standard samples presents a linear correlation in the concentration range of 1–200 μM. The ED-Dose curves were utilized to calibrate the concentrations of the selected samples by substituting their EDs into the formula of the calibration models. As shown in Fig. 6C, the predicted concentrations (PC) of the selected samples of VMA (60 μM and 100 μM) are 60.899 μM and 104.790 μM, respectively. The prediction error rates (PER) present high prediction accuracy (>85%) based on the calibration models. These results confirm the feasibility of quantitative analysis of urinary OAs based on the sensor array. Similarly, the sensing system shows satisfying quantitative analysis results for urinary HVA (Fig. 6D).

It is another significant challenge to quantify two-component samples in a complex sample environment simultaneously. Simultaneous quantitative analysis of two-component OA samples in urine samples has never been accomplished by a simple cross-reactive array. In order to achieve this goal, the sensor array was employed to process a series of HVA and VMA two-component samples in urine. The concentrations of HVA and VMA were set in the range of 0–120 μM and 0–190 μM, respectively. The recorded FL response data were first processed by LDA to build up the sensing model of the two-component VMA-HVA samples. Fig. S5 shows that all of the data points from various two-component VMA-HVA samples are separate with 100% accuracy and present a concentration-dependent distribution. Regression analysis of HVA and VMA were executed by an SVM algorithm for calibrating each OA in the VMA-HVA samples. The acquired eight data sets were divided into two parts, six for calibration and modeling and the other two for cross-validation. The developed model can show the ever-changing laws of data. The concentration of the samples to be tested can also be calculated through the data model. It is clear that a quantitative regression analysis model from the two-component VMA-HVA samples has been developed. The model shows a satisfying calibration performance, and the errors are minimal through visual inspection of the plots of predicted concentration versus actual concentration from HVA and VMA ( Fig. 7). The prediction concentrations of HVA and VMA are incredibly close to their actual concentrations (HVA: 30 μM and 60 μM; VMA: 48 μM and 95 μM) in validation samples. The root-mean-square errors (RMSEs) of calibration (C), cross-validation (CV), and prediction (P) can manifest the correlation coefficients of the calibration. Both the inner cross-validation and external validation are above 85%, indicating the reliability of the prediction models. Limits of detections (LOD) were determined as 0.313 μM for HVA and 0.340 μM for VMA, and the limits of quantification (LOQ) (3.3-fold LOD) were 1.033 μM for HVA and 1.122 μM for VMA. The above results confirm the feasibility of quantitative analysis of OAs in two-component samples, even in complicated environments.

A multichannel fluorescent sensor array based on amino acid-functionalized CDs was designed to analyze urinary OA biomarkers effectively. The CDs can interact directly with diverse OAs through electrostatic and covalent bonding and guarantee the sensitivity and selectivity of the cross-reactive sensor array against various OA targets. This direct-response array system can effectively differentiate seven OA targets over a wide range of concentrations (0.1–1000 μM) with 100% accuracy. The results of double-blind and interference experiments further confirmed the excellent recognition and anti-interference ability of the sensing system. These excellent performances of the sensor array enabled satisfactory discrimination of OAs in one-component or two-component samples with different molar ratios in urine samples. More notably, the facile sensing platform can realize the quantitative analysis of OAs in two-component actual samples. Based on the above results, we believe this array system will exert an enormous function for simultaneous qualitative and quantitative analysis of multiple urinary OA biomarkers and provide convenient tools for the non-invasive diagnosis of OA-related diseases.

Na Du: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Manling Chen: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yunmiao Cui: Investigation. Xue Liu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yang Li: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the authors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873085) and LiaoNing Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC2007056).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130825.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino and organic acid analysis: essential tools in the diagnosis of inborn errors of metabolism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.S. Phipps, P.M. Jones, K. Patel, Amino and organic acid analysis: Essential tools in the diagnosis of inborn errors of metabolism, Adv. Clin. Chem. 92 (2019) 59–103. https://doi.org/10.1016/bs.acc.2019.04.001.

Pradelles, Competitive enzyme immunoassay with monoclonal antibody for homovanillic acid measurement in human urine samples, Clin. Chem. 43 (1997) 363–368. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0009–9120(97)81760–2.

Huang, One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped amphoteric carbon dots for bioimaging from Bombyx mori silk – natural proteins, J. Mater. Chem. B 1 (2013) 2868–2873. https://doi.org/10.1039/c3tb20418a.

Zhao, Rational Design of d-PeT Phenylethynylated-Carbazole Monoboronic Acid Fluorescent Sensors for the Selective Detection of α-Hydroxyl Carboxylic Acids and Monosaccharides, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131 (2009) 17452–17463. https://doi.org/10.1021/ja9060646.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual chromophore sensor for the detection of amines, diols, hydroxy acids, and amino alcohols

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Pushina, S. Farshbaf, E.G. Shcherbakova, P. Anzenbacher, A dual chromophore sensor for the detection of amines, diols, hydroxy acids, and amino alcohols, Chem. Commun. 55 (2019) 4495–4498. https://doi.org/10.1039/c9cc01051c.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insight into the effect of functional groups on visible-fluorescence emissions of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Du, H. Wang, L. Wang, S. Zhu, Y. Song, B. Yang, H. Sun, Insight into the effect of functional groups on visible-fluorescence emissions of graphene quantum dots, J. Mater. Chem. C 4 (2016) 2235–2242. https://doi.org/10.1039/c6tc00548a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sex-linked recessive disorder with retardation of growth, peculiar hair, and focal cerebral and cerebellar degeneration

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.H. Menkes, M. Alter, G.K. Steigleder, D.R. Weakley, J.H. Sung, A sex-linked recessive disorder with retardation of growth, peculiar hair, and focal cerebral and cerebellar degeneration, Pediatrics 29 (1962) 764–779.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis of biomarkers using capillary modification with gold nanoparticles embedded in a polycation and boron doped diamond electrode

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhou, J.D. Glennon, J.H. T. Luong, Electrophoretic Analysis of Biomarkers using Capillary Modification with Gold Nanoparticles Embedded in a Polycation and Boron Doped Diamond Electrode, Anal. Chem. 82 (2010) 6895–6903. https://doi.org/10.1021/ac101105q.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Na Du is working for her M.S. degree from the College of Chemistry, majoring in Chemical Engineering, Liaoning University. She is interested in the preparation of carbon dots.

Manling Chen is working for her Ph.D. degree from the College of Chemistry, majoring in Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Liaoning University. She is interested in the preparation of carbon nanomaterials and research on their related applications.

Xue Liu is a Professor at the College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, China. He received his Ph.D. degree from the College of Chemistry, Nankai University, China, in 2013. Now, he is engaged in the preparation and application of carbon nanomaterials.

Yang Li is a Lecturer at Key Laboratory of Medical Cell Biology of Ministry of Education, China Medical University, China. She received her Ph.D. degree from China Medical University, China, in 2009. Now, she is engaged in oncology.
Yunmiao Cui is working for his B.S. degree in Chemistry, Liaoning University. She is interested in the preparation of carbon nanomaterials.
